_readme: These are GENERAL agronomic principles (nutrient-demand and pest/disease-cycle logic that is widely taught in agricultural science), not yet cross-checked against a specific ICAR/State Agricultural University/KVK publication for Pune district. Treat as a reasonable starting ruleset to validate, not a cited source — flagged verified: false throughout, same discipline as the price/yield data.

families.legume: Nitrogen-fixing (tur, gram, groundnut, other pulses) — replenishes soil nitrogen for the next crop
families.legume_oilseed: Nitrogen-fixing oilseed (groundnut) — similar replenishing effect to legume
families.cereal_grass: Heavy nitrogen feeder (wheat, jowar, rice) — depletes soil nitrogen, benefits strongly from following a legume
families.nightshade: Heavy feeder, shares pest/disease pressure with other nightshades (tomato, brinjal, chili) — repeating or rotating within this family raises disease carryover risk
families.allium: Moderate feeder (onion, garlic) — relatively rotation-neutral, but repeating in the same plot raises soil-borne disease risk over time
families.fiber: Heavy feeder, long-duration (cotton) — significantly depletes soil, strongly benefits from a following legume season
families.sugarcane: Very heavy feeder, long-duration (12-18 months) — typically locks land out of rotation for over a year; soil restoration afterward matters a lot
families.vine_fruit: Perennial (grapes) — not part of annual rotation, land is committed multi-year
families.orchard_fruit: Perennial (pomegranate, fig) — not part of annual rotation, land is committed multi-year
families.banana: Long-duration semi-perennial — heavy feeder, land tied up ~12 months
families.spice_rhizome: Moderate feeder (turmeric) — long duration (~9 months), soil-borne disease risk if repeated

sequencing_rules.description: Do not recommend the same crop family in consecutive seasons on the same plot — raises pest/disease carryover risk, especially for nightshade and allium families.
A legume (or legume_oilseed) following a cereal_grass or fiber crop is preferred — replenishes nitrogen the prior crop depleted.
A cereal_grass or fiber crop following a legume season performs better and needs less fertilizer input — this is the classic 'good' sequence.

vine_fruit, orchard_fruit, and banana commit the land for multiple years — rotation logic does not apply once planted; this should be flagged to the farmer as a long-horizon decision, not a seasonal one.
Sugarcane ties up land for 12-18 months; the season immediately after harvest benefits strongly from a legume before returning to a heavy feeder, given how much sugarcane depletes the soil.

next_step: Before this goes live, validate against an actual ICAR or Maharashtra State Agricultural University (Rahuri / MPKV) cropping-sequence publication for Pune's specific agro-climatic zone, and replace this generic ruleset with district-specific guidance where it differs.
